theme: Your office has amnesia — every missed micro-detail is a missed micro-laugh
visual_observations: Desktop (1280x800): Crushing It / LEGEND tier. Luna at top-left desk with witch-hat character. Bloberto center-top with purple glowing border, CVO label. Carlos and Maya at desks in working state. Dave bottom-left, Sofia center-bottom with red ! error badge. VACANT desk bottom-right — still bare VACANT text. Conference table bottom-left empty; ambient text reads everyone is at their desk. Right wall: COFFEE TRIPLE SHOT cup, cactus, tall amber-orange rectangle (the whiteboard — unidentifiable from distance, no visible label), small green plant, gold sun weather icon. Entrance door at bottom center labeled Entrance. Shortcut toast bottom-right visible. Mobile (375x812): Keyboard shortcut bar renders BELOW the office-floor card boundary — outside the container, floating in document body. Office elements survive breakpoint but shortcut text is exposed and irrelevant on touchscreen.

ideas.title: Kill the Keyboard Shortcut Toast on Mobile
ideas.description: On mobile (375x812), the shortcut toast renders OUTSIDE the office floor boundary — floating below the card in document body. On a touchscreen there is no keyboard, making this broken AND meaningless. Fix: add @media (pointer: coarse) { .shortcut-toast { display: none !important; } } to App.css. Also audit the absolute positioning to be contained within the app root on desktop. WHY: A floating keyboard shortcut hint on a device with no keyboard is textbook broken UI. It is the first debris a mobile user sees below the office floor — and it should simply not exist.

ideas.title: The Whiteboard Needs a Label So It Looks Like One
ideas.description: The whiteboard renders as a tall amber-orange gradient rectangle on the right wall. From desktop viewing distance it looks like a decorative color swatch — there is NO visual affordance that it IS a whiteboard. The VIBE_WHITEBOARD content is only readable on close inspection. Fix: add a masking-tape strip label across the top edge of .office-whiteboard via a CSS ::before pseudo-element: a 3px-high strip in a slightly different hue with the text WHITEBOARD in font-size: 0.45rem; letter-spacing: 0.15em; opacity: 0.5. No JS changes. WHY: An element that looks like a color swatch teaches nobody. One CSS pseudo-element makes it legible as the information-dense vibe panel it actually is.

ideas.title: Conference Table Remembers Its Last Huddle
ideas.description: When a meeting ends (meetingWorkers drops from 2+ to 0), the Huddle badge disappears and the table returns to ambient text — as if the meeting never happened. Add a lastHuddleRef in Office. When meetingWorkers.length >= 2, store { names, endedAt: null }. When it drops to 0, set endedAt: Date.now(). Pass lastHuddle to ConferenceTable and render a muted caption: Last: Dave, Sofia · 4m ago — hidden when null or older than 30 minutes. WHY: The office has no memory. A conference table that shows who last sat there turns a decorative circle into a temporal record without any new API calls.

ideas.title: The Entrance Door Announces by Name
ideas.description: The door shows 🚪✨ on doorEvent===arrive — a visual event with zero information about WHO arrived. App.jsx already fires doorEvent on roster diffs. Extend it: when a worker joins activeWorkers, set doorEvent to { type: arrive, name: worker.name }. In the door JSX, replace static icon with a two-line arrival notice: icon line 1, {name} is here line 2 — in a tiny badge that slides up from the door for 2.5s then fades. Similarly for departures: {name} left. Use data-arrival-name attribute and drive content via existing CSS classes. WHY: 🚪✨ is visual noise. ✨ Carlos is back is a micro-story.

ideas.title: Fern Gets a Visible Nameplate
ideas.description: The office ficus has title=Fern the office ficus — a beautiful detail that 95% of users never discover because browser title tooltips are invisible unless hovered, and the plant is small. Add a .ficus-nameplate div below .ficus-pot: a tiny plant-tag SVG with Fern as text. Style as a 2x0.7rem cream-colored label on a thin green stick at font-size 0.38rem opacity 0.7. When plantStage === blooming, append a flower emoji. No JS changes needed — the class already exists. WHY: Fern has a name, a growth arc, and personality tied to team morale. Hiding her name in a title attribute is a storytelling failure. A visible label turns a background prop into a character.

ideas.title: Vibe Weather Station Forecast Tooltip
ideas.description: The .vibe-weather div renders a single emoji icon in the office floor corner. No label, no text — just an emoji floating in the grid. Add a VIBE_FORECAST constant: crushing = Clear skies 100% shipped, on-fire = Storm warning avoid eye contact, in-flow = Light focus flow state detected, slow-day = Dense fog questionable velocity, after-hours = Night watch 18C eerily quiet. Render as a data-tooltip on the vibe-weather div using the existing CSS tooltip system. On mobile render as a tiny 2-word sub-label below the emoji. WHY: The weather metaphor already exists. Making it speak a forecast doubles the comedy for zero new infrastructure.

ideas.title: Bloberto's Desk Has a Mood Ring Strip
ideas.description: Bloberto manager desk shows: avatar, name, CVO title, glowing border — but no history. Add a row of 5 mini colored dots (.mgr-mood-ring) below the CVO label — each 5x5px, colored by vibe palette, fed by the last 5 entries of vibeHistory[] passed to the manager desk JSX. Dots ordered oldest-to-newest left-to-right with staggered CSS fade-in via --i index. Hover any dot shows a micro-tooltip: Crushing It 4m ago. WHY: Bloberto is the vibe barometer. His desk showing last 5 mood states turns him from a static manager into a living history chart. A volatile strip tells a very different story than 5 calm purple dots. The team mood — rendered on the boss nameplate.
